# **STATEMENT OF ORIGINALITY** {#statement-of-originality}

In my capacity as an active student at President University and as the author of the Capstone Design Project stated below:

Name			: 1\. Athaillah Dea Arkananta – 001202300204

			  3\. Hafidh Bintang Ramadhan – 001202300206

Faculty			: Computer Science

I hereby declare that my Capstone Design Project entitled “**AI-Assisted IT Asset Lifecycle with Behavioral Risk Detection**” is, to the best of my knowledge and belief, an original piece of work based on sound academic principles. If any plagiarism is detected in this final project, I am willing to be personally responsible for the consequences of these acts of plagiarism and will accept the sanctions against these acts in accordance with the rules and policies of President University.

I also declare that this work, either in whole or in part, has not been submitted to another university to obtain a degree.

Cikarang, 2026

# **AI-ASSISTED IT LIFECYCLE WITH BEHAVIORAL RISK DETECTION** {#ai-assisted-it-lifecycle-with-behavioral-risk-detection}

Approved:

|   Rosalina, S.Kom., M.Kom. Capstone Advisor |   Rosalina, S.Kom., M.Kom. Program Head of Informatics |
| ----- | ----- |

Prof. Dr. Ir. Wiranto Herry Utomo, M.Kom.

Dean of the Faculty of Computer Science

# **PROPOSAL** {#proposal}

## **A. PROBLEM FORMULATION** {#a.-problem-formulation}

1. **Background of the Problem**  
   In a company, there is a person who usually handles and manages the inventory assets. Usually, they are referred to as an IT Administrator. Since they are responsible for handling the assets, it is normal for them to hand them over to someone who requests them. However, the moment of handing over the asset is the point at which the responsibility shifts from the IT Administrator, who handles the assets, to the user, the one who borrows it. Sometimes, company policies may not be able to address this issue effectively.  
     
   The core issue is in how the assets' lifecycle is handled. Currently, some companies face issues in these three areas: Firstly, they cannot judge the borrower’s reliability based on their previous history of borrowing, since there is no feature to record the user's history of borrowing. Secondly, they cannot verify the borrower's identity to prevent impersonation. Thirdly, they cannot guarantee that the transaction history has not been altered by someone, especially a malicious administrator.  
     
   This also happens because of the lack of security in the system. Sometimes, companies wait for problems to happen rather than prevent them. If we talk about the users' behavior, the system lacks automation to detect user borrowing history, such as how many times they have returned the asset late or damaged. From the identity perspective, standard handout procedures usually lack a strong non-repudiation system, which means a user can pretend to be someone else, because borrowing an asset does not have strict rules. Lastly, since there is no anti-tampering system in the program, this means it is easier for someone to alter the system’s history logs of borrowing an asset.  
     
   These three vulnerabilities create real-world problems. First, someone who frequently damages or returns the asset late may continue to do so. Secondly, without a proof of the handover, a user can avoid responsibility by claiming they never received the asset at all. Thirdly, a malicious administrator or intruder can easily exploit the system since it lacks an immutable audit trail, making it simple to alter a database column via SQL injection or other means. This is also a problem since it is impossible to track down the person who did it.

2. **Problem from a Customer Perspective**  
   From an operational perspective, the lack of a unified system creates a gap in information for the company. Since no system can analyze the past behavior of the user who borrowed an asset, the system assesses the actual security for the next transaction based on assumptions rather than real data.  
     
   Minimum information forces the team to be more proactive, meaning problems will be handled after they happen. In this current situation, managers often do not discover critical issues, such as negligent users who damaged or returned the assets late, until the financial cost has already been incurred. Without a tool to identify risk before a user borrows an item, especially a negligent one, the company is unable to prevent the loss. By the time the damage has happened, it is too late to recover the cost.  
     
   Furthermore, using standard databases will reduce the integrity of the company’s data. Because someone can easily change the row status in the database, such as making a missing status into returned, and we will not even know who did it. With this current status, the team cannot decide whether an error was an accident or intentional manipulation. This makes the data inaccurate, leading the company to doubt the inventory report it receives.  
     
   Lastly, there is also a security gap regarding identity verification. Without a verified confirmation, the system cannot prove who borrowed the asset. This allows users not be responsible for items that they borrowed or enables unauthorized use by a user to access someone else's equipment with someone else's name. Making expensive hardware and sensitive information can be misused or stolen.  
     
3. **Problems Statement**  
   The problems that occurred in this project in the IT Asset Management lifecycle are:  
1. The system that can analyze the user who wants to borrow an item.  
2. The lack of proof during the handover of an item.  
3. A malicious person who can tamper with the database.

4. **Objectives**  
   The objectives of this Capstone project are:  
1. To analyze the user behavior with the help of AI.  
2. To secure the handover of an item with “digital signing”.  
3. To alert and prevent someone from trying to tamper with the database.

## **B. PROBLEM ANALYSIS** {#b.-problem-analysis}

Based on the previous Problem Statements, we look deeper into the reasons behind these issues and how they affect us. We break down the problem into specific areas to understand what we really need to fix.

**Aspect 1: Operational Efficiency and Workflow**  
When we look at how IT works, the main issue is that the current process relies on manual work and trust. This means the IT administrator works based on trust rather than data. This is not efficient because it is almost impossible for a human being to remember all of the employees in the company. Maybe they can if the company has fewer than 50 people, but what if it were an international company with more than 10,000 employees? It would take ages and slow down the IT team if the administrator needs to manually check it using old history data. We need to think about how to change this workflow from a reactive one to a proactive one. The problem with the current system is that we need to wait to know if the laptop that someone borrowed is broken or not. A good system should be able to analyze a pattern in the data history, making it able to prevent an accident before it happens. If we do not fix this problem immediately, the IT team will be stuck checking the user data history one-by-one.

**Aspect 2: Data Integrity and Security**  
This part analyzes the weakness of the storage system that is being used. The problem is that standard databases are quite poor in terms of integrity. Currently, if a malicious administrator or a hacker has access, they can use a simple command line or even the entire database. They could change the item status from missing to returned, and the system won't give a notification or warning about it at all. This is because of the lack of an audit trail. We also need to analyze the security of the handover process. Without strong proof or a proper signature as verification, the system cannot determine if the user identity is the same as the one who requested it. This makes it easier for someone to lie or steal someone's identity. We need to figure out how to increase the integrity of the data, ensuring that the data is really safe from outsiders, as well as those who handle or create it.

**Aspect 3: Financial Risk and Asset Protection**  
From a financial perspective, the lack of a smart system is the cause of financial loss for the company. We need to analyze the cost of negligence. When a negligent user who has broken a company asset previously, even often, there is a chance that it may happen again. The current system does not have a way to calculate this risk, so there is a high chance that this will cause the company to lose money over and over again. We are not only talking about the hardware cost that the company needs to replace, but also the accuracy of the company's wealth report. If the database can be easily tampered with, then the data that the managers read might be wrong. They might believe that some of the assets are actually gone. This leads to bad budgeting and spending. We need to analyze how to stop these financial leaks by ensuring that the inventory number is always real and accurate.

## **C. SOLUTION SELECTION & SCENARIOS** {#c.-solution-selection-&-scenarios}

1. **Alternative Solutions**  
* **Solution 1**  
  Manually improve the basic database. The first alternative solution is to maintain the current workflow, which is manually, but upgrading the storage into a more centralized database, such as MySQL or Microsoft Access. We are simply giving the IT administrator a better platform to input data. We plan to add a simple "Black List" column in the database. When a user comes to borrow an asset, the IT administrator has to manually input and change the database, checking the user's history row by row, and then decide whether to lend the asset or not based on their own assumption. For security, we would only implement a standard login page with a little complexity password requirement, such as case sensitive and a minimum length.

  The problem with this solution is that it still relies heavily on manual human work. The database is just like a storage box; it does not provide anything at all, just waiting for a new query to be input. So human error is highly possible to happen if the administrator is tired, biased, or even if they are feeling lazy and skip checking the history, so that the process will not take long, and just hand over the asset. Also, a standard database without encryption or blockchain-like hashing is straightforward to manipulate. If someone can breach and gain the authority of an admin, they can just manipulate the history records, and no one would notice because there is no system watching the logs. This solution fixes the storage and is also really easy and cheap to implement, but it still lacks in solving the core problems of human error and data tampering.

* **Solution 2**  
  Custom rule-based application (Non-AI). The second alternative is to develop a custom web-based application that uses hard-coded programming logic, basically a lot of unnecessary conditions. Instead of using AI to learn patterns, we implement the rule directly in the code. For example, we implement a code rule saying: “if the user is late more than 3 months, then reject”. It is like an upgrade for alternative solution 1 since it has automation. The admin does not need to check the history of the user manually because the codes do it for them.

* **Solution 3**  
  System with AI and Immutable Asset Audit Trail. The third alternative solution is developing a system that integrates a Random Forest Machine Learning for dynamic risk analysis and an unchangeable cryptographic asset audit trail for security. Unlike the previous one, this solution does not use hard-coded code. Instead, it uses a trained AI model to calculate the "Risk Score" based on the user's history of handover. Not only that, it can tell a difference between a slightly careless user and a dangerous one by considering factors such as usage time, damage frequency, and how many times the user returns the asset on time.

  On the security side, this solution is not only by using a standard password. We plan to implement "Digital Handshake" by using Cryptographic signing during the handover, making sure the user can not deny that they are the one who is borrowing the asset. Furthermore, to prevent the database tampering problem, we plan to implement a hashing mechanism that connects the Transaction Activity Logs. If any bad IT administration tries to tamper with the record to clean the user's history, the hashing chain will break, resulting in the system triggering the security alarm.

2. **Solution Selection**

Based on the 3 solutions we considered, we are choosing solution 3\. We chose this solution because it is the only one that truly solves the “Human Factor” and “Trust” problems. Alternative 1 is rejected because it only focuses on upgrading the database, not fixing the human error problem. The admin can still make bad decisions. Also, the high risk of being tampered with by hackers. Alternative 2 is rejected because it does automate things, but it is not flexible. Human behavior and mind are too complex for simple “if-else” rules. We need a system that can calculate different variables dynamically, which is why the combination of AI and Cyber Security is highly reasonable.

Furthermore, from a security perspective, alternative 3 is the only one that offers “Non-Repudiation” (via digital signing) and “Anti-Tampering” (via log hashing). Since our problem statement highlighted the risk of malicious insiders changing the data, we need the cryptographic features in alternative 3, which cannot be found in the other two alternatives.

3. **Solution Usage Scenarios**

In a company, there is a corrupt or a bad staff member who tries to sneak into the IT department and tries to access the database to delete the record of a laptop. The staff uses an SQL tool to delete the row. However, the system is built on an unchangeable asset audit trail by using a chain-hashing method for the database, making it more secure. Because the row is deleted, the system will detect an authorized change, even if they are an administrator. If the new hash does not match the previous hash, the system will detect it as an anomaly and give an alert status. The attempt to tamper with the database fails, and the history remains unchanged.

## **D. DEVELOPMENT EFFORT** {#d.-development-effort}

| Hafidh Bintang Ramadhan | Contribute to filling in the documents, in charge of the AI Behavioral Risk Detection (AI layer). |

- MySQL  
- Python 3.12  
- Google Colab

# **REFERENCES** {#references}

Breiman, L. (2001). Random Forests. *Machine Learning*, 45(1), 5–32. [https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1010933404324](https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1010933404324)

Suhadolnik, N., Ueyama, J., & Da Silva, S. (2023). Machine Learning for Enhanced Credit Risk Assessment: An Empirical Approach. *Journal of Risk and Financial Management*, 16(12), 496\.  
[https://www.mdpi.com/1911-8074/16/12/496](https://www.mdpi.com/1911-8074/16/12/496)

Pavani, K., & Pradeep, K. (2015). Forensic Analysis of Database using Secure Audit Log. *International Journal of Latest Trends in Engineering and Technology (IJLTEMAS)*, 4(2), 89-91.  
[https://www.ijltemas.in/DigitalLibrary/Vol.4Issue2/89-91.pdf](https://www.ijltemas.in/DigitalLibrary/Vol.4Issue2/89-91.pdf)

Snodgrass, R. T., Yao, S. S., & Collberg, C. (2004). Tamper detection in audit logs. *Proceedings of the 30th VLDB Conference*, 148–159.  
[https://www.vldb.org/conf/2004/RS13P1.PDF](https://www.vldb.org/conf/2004/RS13P1.PDF)

Reason, J. (2000). Human error: models and management. Bmj, 320(7237), 768-770.

Sheridan, T. B. (2008). Risk, human error, and system resilience: fundamental ideas. Human factors, 50(3), 418-426.
